overview: The Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (VFV.TO) tracks the S&P 500 Index, providing Canadian investors with exposure to the 500 largest U.S. publicly traded companies. VFV is denominated in Canadian dollars but is UNHEDGED — meaning investors have full exposure to USD/CAD currency fluctuations. When the U.S. dollar strengthens against the Canadian dollar, VFV benefits; when it weakens, returns are reduced. The hedged version is VSP.TO. VFV holds the same underlying stocks as the iconic U.S.-listed VOO, making it one of the most popular ETFs on the TSX for passive U.S. equity exposure. With an MER of just 0.09%, it is one of the lowest-cost ways for Canadians to invest in the S&P 500.
bullCase: Ultra-low MER of 0.09%, making it one of the cheapest S&P 500 ETFs available in Canada.
Provides diversified exposure to 500 of the largest U.S. companies, reducing individual stock risk.
Unhedged structure benefits Canadian investors when the USD strengthens relative to CAD.
bearCase: Unhedged — full USD/CAD currency exposure means returns can be dragged down when the Canadian dollar strengthens.
Performance is closely tied to the U.S. market, which may be volatile.
Dividend payments are subject to U.S. withholding tax (15% in RRSP, 15-25% in TFSA/non-registered).
analystSummary: VFV is widely regarded as the go-to unhedged S&P 500 ETF for Canadian investors. Its ultra-low 0.09% MER and broad U.S. equity exposure make it a core holding in many portfolios. Investors should understand the currency exposure — VFV does NOT hedge USD/CAD, unlike its sibling VSP.TO.
seoDescription: Explore whether to buy VFV with our comprehensive analysis of the Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (CAD) — unhedged U.S. equity exposure at 0.09% MER.
